topic.topic: Can agent memory improve generalization or does it create retrieval interference?

topic_summary: tree_search_scientist research package for: Can agent memory improve generalization or does it create retrieval interference?

primitive_definitions.name: Experience Memory
primitive_definitions.definition: A storage system that retains past agent interactions (state, action, outcome) for future retrieval.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is a fundamental building block of memory-augmented agents, cannot be decomposed further in this context.

primitive_definitions.name: Retrieval Interference
primitive_definitions.definition: A phenomenon where similar retrieval cues activate conflicting memory traces, degrading performance.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is a core cognitive effect that emerges from memory retrieval dynamics, serving as a key explanatory concept.

primitive_definitions.name: Generalization
primitive_definitions.definition: The ability to apply learned knowledge to novel tasks or variations not seen during training.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is a primary goal of learning systems and directly measures the utility of memory beyond rote recall.

assumptions.assumption: Compression of experiences into semantic memory retains task-relevant patterns while discarding surface-level details.
assumptions.rationale: This is grounded in cognitive science where semantic memory abstracts commonalities across episodes.
assumptions.possible_failure: Compression may lose critical details needed for specific tasks, causing semantic memory to underperform.

assumptions.assumption: Retrieval interference arises primarily from exact-match or similarity-based retrieval of raw episodic traces.
assumptions.rationale: Prior work shows that storing raw experiences leads to conflict when similar contexts require different actions.
assumptions.possible_failure: Interference may be negligible even with episodic memory, or may stem from other factors like capacity limits.

variables.name: topic condition
variables.description: The main condition or task distribution studied by the baseline.

variables.name: proposed mechanism
variables.description: Semantic memory compresses multiple episodic experiences into a compact representation (e.g., via summarization or clustering), which reduces the overlap between stored traces and thus minimizes retrieval interference. This allows the agent to generalize better to paraphrased instructions by relying on abstract patterns rather than specific past instances.

variables.name: research quality
variables.description: Whether the proposed question is falsifiable and experimentable.

causal_chain.step: Semantic memory compresses multiple episodic experiences into a compact representation (e.g., via summarization or clustering), which reduces the overlap between stored traces and thus minimizes retrieval interference. This allows the agent to generalize better to paraphrased instructions by relying on abstract patterns rather than specific past instances.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: Semantic memory compresses multiple episodic experiences into a compact representation (e.g., via summarization or clustering), which reduces the overlap between stored traces and thus minimizes retrieval interference. This allows the agent to generalize better to paraphrased instructions by relying on abstract patterns rather than specific past instances.

tensions.statement: Adding memory to an agent may improve performance by leveraging past experiences, but can also create interference when similar contexts map to conflicting solutions, harming generalization. Semantic memory aims to resolve this by compressing experiences to retain only generalizable patterns.
tensions.why_it_matters: Prior work on memory-augmented LLMs has explored episodic and semantic memory separately, but few studies directly compare their effect on generalization under controlled interference conditions. This experiment isolates the role of compression in reducing interference while controlling for retrieval and capacity.

candidate_questions.question: Does semantic memory based on compression improve generalization in instruction-following agents compared to episodic memory or no memory?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: Prior work on memory-augmented LLMs has explored episodic and semantic memory separately, but few studies directly compare their effect on generalization under controlled interference conditions. This experiment isolates the role of compression in reducing interference while controlling for retrieval and capacity.

tension_or_contradiction.statement: Adding memory to an agent may improve performance by leveraging past experiences, but can also create interference when similar contexts map to conflicting solutions, harming generalization. Semantic memory aims to resolve this by compressing experiences to retain only generalizable patterns.
tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: Prior work on memory-augmented LLMs has explored episodic and semantic memory separately, but few studies directly compare their effect on generalization under controlled interference conditions. This experiment isolates the role of compression in reducing interference while controlling for retrieval and capacity.

research_question.question: Does semantic memory based on compression improve generalization in instruction-following agents compared to episodic memory or no memory?
research_question.expected_contribution: Prior work on memory-augmented LLMs has explored episodic and semantic memory separately, but few studies directly compare their effect on generalization under controlled interference conditions. This experiment isolates the role of compression in reducing interference while controlling for retrieval and capacity.

hypothesis.statement: An agent with semantic memory will achieve higher success rates on paraphrased instructions than an episodic-memory agent, and will approach or exceed the performance of a no-memory baseline.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: Semantic memory leads to a 15-25% relative improvement in generalization accuracy over episodic memory, with no significant increase in interference.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: Implement three agents: (1) No memory: zero-shot LLM with in-context examples. (2) Episodic memory: k-NN retrieval from raw experience buffer. (3) Semantic memory: experiences are summarized via LLM every N steps and stored as compressed representations; retrieval uses similarity to these summaries. All agents use the same underlying LLM (e.g., Llama 2-7B). Test on a set of instruction-following tasks (e.g., ALFWorld, WebShop) with paraphrased variants. Each agent trains on 100 episodes per task, then evaluated on 50 new paraphrases. Measure success rate, interference rate (defined as performance drop on ambiguous contexts), and memory size. Repeat 5 random seeds. Compare means with t-tests.
dependent_variables: Interference rate (performance drop on ambiguous contexts)
controls: Same underlying LLM for all agents
Equal number of stored experiences (or equivalent computational budget)
Same task set and paraphrase generation process
Retrieval method (k-NN with k=5 for both memory types)
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If semantic memory does not significantly outperform episodic memory on generalization (p > 0.05) or if it performs worse than no memory, the hypothesis is falsified.

supports_hypothesis: Semantic memory yields significantly higher success rates than episodic memory on paraphrased tasks.
Interference rate is lower for semantic memory compared to episodic memory.
Semantic memory requires less storage than episodic memory.
rejects_hypothesis: Semantic memory performs similarly to episodic memory (no significant difference).
Semantic memory performs worse than no memory due to loss of detail.

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If semantic memory fails to improve over episodic memory, it suggests that compression either discards important information or that interference is not the primary bottleneck. This would shift focus to retrieval mechanisms or memory capacity. If it underperforms no memory, it indicates that any memory (even compressed) introduces harmful bias, supporting the hypothesis that memory creates interference despite compression.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: Compression of experiences into semantic memory retains task-relevant patterns while discarding surface-level details.
failure_update_rule.next_question: What mechanism explains failure of: Does semantic memory based on compression improve generalization in instruction-following agents compared to episodic memory or no memory?

branches.mechanism_bet: Episodic memory retrieval causes interference when similar contexts map to conflicting past solutions, harming generalization.
branches.minimal_experiment: Compare an LLM agent with episodic memory (k-NN retrieval) vs. no memory on a text-based game with varying parameters; measure generalization accuracy.
branches.expected_failure_mode: Memory may not cause significant interference due to robust retrieval, yielding negligible effect.

branches.mechanism_bet: Semantic memory that compresses experiences reduces interference and improves generalization by capturing patterns.
branches.minimal_experiment: Implement agent with memory buffer that distills experiences into semantic memory (clustering/summarization); test on instruction following with paraphrases; compare to no memory and episodic memory.
branches.expected_failure_mode: Compression may lose critical details, causing failure on tasks needing specific knowledge.

branches.mechanism_bet: Interference is due to faulty retrieval, not memory itself; better retrieval (e.g., attention-based) improves generalization.
branches.minimal_experiment: Compare simple nearest-neighbor vs. attention-based learned retrieval over memory slots on multi-step reasoning tasks; measure success rate and interference rate.
branches.expected_failure_mode: Complex retrieval may be too costly or overfit to training tasks, showing no advantage.

selection.selection_rationale: B002 directly tests the core question of whether memory improves generalization or creates interference by comparing semantic (compressed) memory to episodic memory. The mechanism (compression reduces interference) is clear and novel, the experiment is feasible using existing summarization/clustering techniques, and the expected failure mode (loss of detail) provides clear falsifiability. B001 is also relevant but less novel; B003 focuses on retrieval design rather than the memory type itself.
expansion_instructions: Define specific semantic memory compression methods: e.g., running summarization via LLM, clustering similar experiences and storing centroids, or using a variational autoencoder.
Implement a baseline episodic memory agent with simple k-NN retrieval from a raw experience buffer.
Implement a no-memory baseline (zero-shot or in-context learning only).
Design a suite of instruction-following tasks with paraphrased variants (e.g., ALFWorld, WebShop, or custom grid-based tasks) to test generalization.
Measure: success rate on new paraphrases, interference rate (performance drop when similar contexts have conflicting optimal actions), and memory usage/size.
Control for task distribution, number of experiences, and retrieval hyperparameters.
Run at least 5 seeds per condition and report statistical significance.
Expected failure: if compression loses too much detail, semantic memory may underperform; if not, it will outperform episodic memory and approach no-memory in generalization.
